speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandmother with my two aunts. She was in her 80s when she was taken to Auschwitz with her husband. And those-- two of her daughters were not survived also. One of them, on the-- the one who-- who's got a-- a kerchief on the right, she was married. And she was pregnant when she was taken to Auschwitz. And I-- I-- what I understand, the baby was born in the camp but didn't survive. And the other one also, unmarried, she didn't survive the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my parents. Both of them were taken to gas chambers in Auschwitz. He was 54, and my mother was 46. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The extensive family-- from my mother's, the only survivor was the uncle, her brother who lived in London. He survived the war. And from my father's family, there was a survivor, a younger-- a younger brother, who went to Mauthausen. And he came back to Auschwitz. And he died just about 15 years ago in Kapuvar. And he's-- was buried there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you have the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this has been-- the history of this photograph-- it has been found in-- in our-- in our home place. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my picture after the war. I think it must have been taken 1948, when I-- when I learned in the yeshiva. I was, at that time, 17 or 18. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It looks a sad picture. I can see it on the expression of the face. Possibly it's-- at that time I have not recovered from the experience, which was a very difficult thing to recover, even in later years.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1981 on my son's bar mitzvah. Those three beautiful girls are my daughters. At that time, they were not married. And from the right, is my oldest brother, my father-in-law, who died since; myself, and my bar mitzvah son, who is-- who got married last year; my wife, and my late mother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken last year, in 19-- 1994-- my son's wedding. And you can see another extended family. The first, from left to right, is my son-in-law, in Israel, and his wife, my daughter, Sarah, with his little boy-- their little boy. The next couple is my daughter, Rachel, who lives here in London. And the little boy and the little girl in front of them are their children-- two among the four. The-- another two one-- they're younger. Then myself and the happy couple; my daughter-in-law, Helen, and my son, Moshe; and then, in the blue, is my wife; and then, my oldest daughter with her husband, who lives in New York, with their two daughters and little boy. Since then, they got another little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son on -- on his wedding day, in Manchester. And that's how I look-- quite happy-- a happy father on the-- on his son's wedding. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is my-- the children of my daughter in New York-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --my-- my daughter and-- and three of her children-- the three girls. And they got a little boy who is not on the picture. It's taken in Long Island, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK, there. Off we go. [PAUSES FOR 22 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Baldy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She's losing her hair. [PAUSES FOR 33 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: When do you think that tape will be ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: In about two to three months you should have a cut.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two to three months, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, whenever you're ready.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Judith. We are happily married now for 35 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: 36, I think. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: You're asking me--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's even better.

speaker: interviewee
text: --a little bit-- he's not sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: 36 years. And this is my youngest daughter, Rachelle-- nearest to us, both in--

speaker: interviewee
text: What about Avita?

speaker: interviewee
text: --just gradual things. And we are coming to it. This is the youngest of the grandchildren, Avita-- the sweetest things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we are expecting another two-- please, God-- soon.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the time being, she's the youngest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. For the time being.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her title is going to be taken away from her very soon-- within a month.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she'll be the youngest in her family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the moment. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only thing that I can think of at the moment is that I never knew one set of grandparents. I was always brought up with having just one set of grandparents. The other set, I never knew. And I missed out on knowing another uncle, who-- who didn't survive the war. Other than that, I had quite a normal upbringing. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what to say. Maybe there's too much to-- to say, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't think.
